next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The charting system at Lakeview was a hybrid — half-digital, half paper — that nobody had fully committed to in either direction. Carmen sat at the Wing A sub-station at 2:37 AM, entering vital signs from her 1 AM rounds into the computer while the paper backup waited on a clipboard beside her. The terminal was old enough that it hummed. The screen cast a blue-white wash over her hands and the laminate counter, which was peeling at the corner where someone had picked at it during hundreds of similar overnight hours.

She'd taken the double for the same reason she always did. The car insurance was due in nine days and she was $140 short. The overnight differential was $3.50 more per hour, which meant the sixteen extra hours bought her the gap plus gas money if she didn't eat out. She hadn't eaten since the turkey sandwich from the vending machine at 10 PM, and she could feel the deficit in her concentration, a lag between noticing something on the screen and understanding what it said.

The building made its night sounds around her. The HVAC cycled on with a low shudder she felt in the counter surface before she heard it. Room 110 — Mrs. Okafor, ninety-one, end-stage COPD — produced the same thin, rhythmic moan she'd been producing for the seven months Carmen had worked this wing, a sound Carmen had stopped hearing the way you stop hearing a refrigerator. A toilet flushed somewhere in B Wing, probably Devon using the staff bathroom, which meant he was at least awake, which was an improvement over last Tuesday when she'd found him face-down on his folded arms at...
open_hooks_to_carry: Harry gets out of his wheelchair and walks — the physical transformation
Ring meeting mechanics: Agnes sorting pills with pharmaceutical precision, Earl counting cash, Lola on lookout
prev_tail_excerpt:
...k.

She woke the station monitor and opened the overnight nursing notes. For Lola she wrote the smallest true thing she had: resident found awake in TV room at 0115, redirected to room, no distress noted. Then she checked 114A and 116A, found Harry and Earl back in bed, and put the same narrow truth in each chart: awake in common area during rounds, in room on recheck, no injury, no acute complaint.

That was chartable. The part that mattered wasn't. She had not heard a single clean sentence, had no papers in her hand, no witness besides Devon asleep in the next chair with his mouth open. If she called Pat at one in the morning and said three residents who spent all day in some version of confusion had been holding a meeting in the dark, Pat would hear the count discrepancy first and the story second. Eight missing controlled pills under Carmen's license, and now a report she could not defend beyond I know what I saw. That was how you got yourself treated like the problem before you had anything solid enough to prove there was one.

So she didn't file an incident report. She didn't page the DON. She left the minimal notes in the chart because they were true, and kept the rest in her head, where it felt less defensible and somehow more dangerous. She sat in the nurse's station and listened to Devon breathe and thought about what she'd seen and tried to build a version of events that didn't require the people she was paid to care for to be something other than what their charts said they were. She tried for a long time. The building hummed. The toilet ran. She couldn't do it.

setups_to_plant.description: Burt's Hungarian-English dictionary — a gift from his wife — is on his shelf and mentioned as meaningful to him; it is the pill stash's hiding place
Earl coughs during the meeting — a deep, wet rattle that he waves off; the first sign of his undiagnosed illness

state_deltas_required: the ring's operational demands and their awareness that Carmen is getting suspicious
the reader sees the full operation — its precision, its domestic absurdity, its human warmth — and learns Harry has identified Carmen as a threat
